_about: Build Nervous System (Stage 4, 'Land dark') client-side feature-flag manifest. Maps a flag name to { enabled, addedInPR, description }. Read once at init time by js/build/build-flags.js into an in-memory object — no localStorage, cookies, network, or server — so it behaves identically in the browser bundle, the Tauri desktop webview, and any future Tauri mobile build. Flag hygiene follows the promote-or-delete rule in docs/build-nervous-system.md: a flag left here for more than 3 merged PRs without being promoted (removed, code kept) or reverted (removed, code deleted) must be flagged in the 4th PR that touches this manifest. `addedInPR` records where each flag's clock started.

glowCanvas.description: Glow Canvas (Batch 1 of N): the first step toward a real multi-chart dashboard, replacing the single-chart-only limitation of the Visualize tab (js/runtimes-viz/visualize.js can only draw ONE chart at a time onto the single #viz-chart node). js/runtimes-viz/glow-canvas.js ships the PURE, Node-testable layout algebra — createCanvasLayout() (an empty {cards,nextId} layout), addCard() (append a card, auto-assigning a monotonic id and a next-available-slot gridPos in a fixed 2-column grid), removeCard(), updateCardPosition(), and serializeLayout()/deserializeLayout() for JSON save/reload where malformed JSON returns a safe empty layout and NEVER throws — every mutator returns a NEW layout and never mutates its input, exactly the pure-core discipline of js/rooms/room-ui.js. Saved layouts persist via a new 'canvasLayouts' object store added to the existing IndexedDB memory store (js/learning/memory-store.js, DB bumped v5->v6, keyed by layout name; saveCanvasLayout/getCanvasLayout/listCanvasLayouts/deleteCanvasLayout/clearCanvasLayouts) — no new storage mechanism invented. The thin browser-only renderer renderCanvas() draws each card as a titled tile on a CSS grid and delegates every actual chart draw to the EXISTING viz.renderChart (reused, never reimplemented), with a per-card Remove affordance and a minimal inline 'Add chart' form; it is left to the browser/e2e path like room-ui.js's renderRoomUi(). This batch DELIBERATELY does NOT add cross-filtering, drag-and-drop reordering, or any wiring into / replacement of the existing Visualize tab — those are later batches, and visualize.js's two existing exports are untouched. Ships OFF: with this flag off (its shipped default) the 'glowcanvas' tab is never added to the bar (see main.js renderTabBar), is never a dead click target, #panel-glowcanvas stays empty (see renderGlowCanvasTab), and the whole app shell is byte-for-byte unchanged.
glowCanvas.promotedNote: Phase 6: Glow Canvas promoted live. The multi-chart dashboard is now the default experience alongside the single-chart Visualize tab.

drillFloor.description: Drill Floor (Batch 1 of N): a practice-problem module in the Maven Analytics 'Data Drill' format where the SAME real problem is solved side-by-side in SQL, Python and R against a bundled sample dataset, so a learner can compare idioms across all three languages against identical data. Batch 1 ships ONE drill, 'Spot the Sale' — join a promos table to an orders table where order_date falls BETWEEN a promo's start_date and end_date (inclusive), i.e. 'which orders happened during an active promo period'. js/drill-floor/drill-floor-data.js is the PURE, Node-testable data layer: a seeded deterministic PRNG generates ~300 sample orders and 14 promos with realistic overlapping/boundary-adjacent date ranges (generateOrders/generatePromos), pure SQL builders (sqlLiteral/buildCreateTableSql) emit atomic CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE + INSERT text, and the only side-effecting function loadDrillTables({runQuery}) delegates to the EXISTING duckdb-engine.runQuery to create two dedicated temp tables drill_orders/drill_promos (namespaced so they NEVER collide with or overwrite the user's own loaded tables) and returns dataset descriptors. js/drill-floor/drill-floor.js holds the DRILLS registry (id/title/difficulty/description/starterSql/starterPython/starterR/expectedApproach), getDrill(), extractRowCount(), and thin run* delegators (runDrillSql/runDrillPython/runDrillR) that reuse the EXISTING runtime bridges (runQuery/runPython/runR) and follow never-throw-out discipline — a runtime rejection becomes a returned {error} field, never a thrown exception. The drill tables are registered into state.datasets (without changing the active dataset) so the existing Python/R pandas/df bridges expose them via dataglow.get_df / dataglow_get_df. The browser-only tab renders drill title/description/difficulty and three side-by-side editable editors pre-filled with each language's starter code, each with a Run button showing stdout/result/row-count/error. Python and R runtimes are HEAVY (Pyodide/WebR); they are NOT initialized when the tab opens — only lazily on the first click of each language's Run button (reusing ensurePythonRuntime/ensureRRuntime). Batch 2 (js/drill-floor/drill-diff.js) is ALSO included and live behind this same flag: a pure cross-language result-diff engine (parseMatchedRows/compareDrillResults/suggestLikelyCause) that compares SQL/Python/R row counts and surfaces a grounded, never-invented likely-cause hint when they disagree — rendered as the Comparison panel beneath the three editors. Still does NOT include additional drills beyond 'Spot the Sale', or any persistence of attempts/progress (a future batch). Promoted to live 2026-07-18 after pre-flight found and fixed two bugs: (1) the Command Deck sidebar was not flag-gating tabs at all (a separate, pre-existing bug affecting any tab with a TAB_META entry, discovered via this flag's OFF-state check — fixed by extracting a shared getVisibleTabIds() gate used by both the sidebar and the top tab bar), and (2) the SQL run output rendered the literal text [object Object] instead of nothing extra (renderDrillOutput was stringifying the raw DuckDB result object as a fallback display string — fixed to only render the result field as text when it is genuinely a string).

cleaningCrew.description: The DataGlow Cleaning Crew (Batch 1 of N): the first slice of an on-device, multi-agent ingestion pipeline (Profiler -> Extractor -> Cleaner -> Validator -> Documenter). Batch 1 ships ONLY the PROFILER station, for exactly ONE new format: PDF text extraction via PDF.js. js/cleaning-crew/pdf-profiler.js is the module: the PURE, Node-testable core takes ALREADY-EXTRACTED per-page text as plain data -- summarizePdfProfile() computes {pageCount, pagesWithText, pagesWithoutText, extractedText, hasExtractableText, warnings}; pdfProfileToRows() shapes it into one row per page (columns page_number/text); buildPdfGateLayers()/evaluatePdfReadiness() translate the profile into the EXISTING js/gate/readiness-gate.js verdict (computeReadinessGate/explainGateReasons), where zero extractable text across all pages is a hard gate FAIL and some-but-not-all pages missing text is a gate PASS with a warning. The browser-only ensurePdfjs()/profilePdf() lazily load the PDF.js v3 UMD build from the jsDelivr CDN on the FIRST PDF profiled only -- never on tab open or app load -- mirroring python-runtime.js's loadPyodideScript()/initPyodideRuntime() pattern, and parse entirely client-side in a Web Worker so a PDF's contents never leave the browser (zero cloud calls, no AI API keys). js/app-shell/loaders.js gains ONE new branch in the same loadFile() format-detection dispatch (ext === 'pdf' -> loadPdfAsDataset()), which extracts text and ingests it via the EXISTING loadRowsAsDataset() so a PDF becomes an ordinary queryable local dataset exactly like a CSV/JSON upload; existing formats are byte-for-byte unchanged, and -- matching every other loadFile branch -- this user-initiated import does NOT route through the Agent Action Firewall. The browser-only Cleaning Crew tab shows the Profiler station name/icon, a PDF upload that reuses the SAME loadPdfAsDataset code path (no second file-input system), and the profile summary (page counts, warnings) plus the readiness-gate verdict. This batch DELIBERATELY does NOT include OCR for scanned pages (Tesseract.js), audio (Whisper), the Extractor/Cleaner/Validator/Documenter stations, semantic chunking/embeddings/vector stores, or any persistence of past runs -- all future batches. Ships OFF: with this flag off (its shipped default) the 'cleaningcrew' tab is never added to the bar, is never a dead click target, #panel-cleaningcrew stays empty (see renderCleaningCrewTab), and the whole app shell -- including CSV/JSON upload -- is byte-for-byte unchanged.

aiTouchLedger.description: AI Touch Ledger (Batch 1 + Batch 2, PROMOTED TO ON): a hash-chained, tamper-evident log of every time an AI model touches a dataset — which model, whether the call stayed on-device or left the browser, which fields/columns it was shown, and what human action triggered it. Fills a gap none of DataGlow's existing provenance/trust modules cover: js/narrative/story.js's generateStory() genuinely forks between an on-device path (WebLLM/Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct, zero network egress) and an external-provider path (OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini, which embeds real query-result rows in an HTTP request body to a third-party endpoint) and now records which path was taken, for which columns, and when. js/provenance/ai-touch-ledger.js: createTouchLedger() returns a session-scoped hash chain (logTouch/getEntries/clear) that reuses the EXACT sha256Hex primitive from js/provenance/provenance.js — no new crypto library or scheme; verifyTouchLedger() re-derives every entry's hash from its recorded fields and confirms it still matches, so any post-hoc edit, deletion, or reordering breaks the chain at the first altered link; summarizeTouchLedger() and exportTouchLedger() (json/markdown/text) mirror the Assumption Ledger's summary/export contract. logTouch() NEVER throws: a malformed touch (missing model, invalid location, missing sentTo for an external call) is recorded as a clearly-marked rejected entry rather than either crashing the real AI call site it observes or being silently dropped. Honest naming inherited from every other provenance module: NOT a zero-knowledge proof, NOT 'blockchain', NOT a certification of AI output accuracy — it attests only that these touches happened, in this order, and the log has not been silently altered since. Batch 2 (PR #206) wired js/narrative/story.js's generateStory() to log every real touch via logStoryTouch(), added the Story-tab UI panel in js/app-shell/main.js + index.html, and added the sixth Proof Room step in js/provenance/proof-room.js. Now live by default: the Story tab shows the AI Touch Ledger panel, and the Proof Room's sixth step is ready.

roomsSignaling.description: DataGlow Rooms (Batch 1 of 4): the collaboration concept that lets an analyst, a data scientist, and a data engineer open the SAME loaded dataset from their own browsers and see each other's SQL/Python/R results and Object Space entries appear live, peer-to-peer, with zero server and zero upload. Batch 1 ships ONLY the pure, Node-testable room-code + peer-discovery/signaling module js/rooms/room-signaling.js: generateRoomCode()/normalizeRoomCode()/isValidRoomCode() and RoomSignalingCoordinator (join/listPeers/leave), reusing the EXACT dependency-injection pattern already proven by js/federated/federated-transport.js (a NULL_ROOM_SIGNALING no-op adapter makes 'unreachable' a first-class, never-thrown state; real adapters are injected in the browser, fakes in tests). NO UI and NO Object Space broadcasting/wiring into the SQL/Python/R tabs yet — that is Batch 2. Ships OFF: with this flag off, nothing in the app imports or calls into js/rooms/room-signaling.js, so every existing path is byte-for-byte unchanged.

roomsBroadcast.description: DataGlow Rooms (Batch 2 of 4): puts the first real payload on the WebRTC data channel Batch 1 opened. When a Room is active, new/updated Object Space entries (js/app-shell/object-space.js) are broadcast to the other peers and each peer's read-only 'who's viewing' tags are tracked. Batch 2 ships ONLY the pure, Node-testable broadcast module js/rooms/room-broadcast.js: buildEntryMessage()/buildViewingMessage() and RoomBroadcastCoordinator (broadcastEntry/broadcastViewing/receive + the viewersOf/objectsViewedBy/viewingSnapshot who's-viewing map), COMPOSING Batch 1's RoomSignalingCoordinator with a data-channel transport ADAPTER that reuses federated-transport.js's WebRTC mesh primitive (a NULL_ROOM_TRANSPORT no-op adapter keeps 'unreachable' a first-class, never-thrown state; real adapter injected in the browser, fakes in tests). Broadcast payloads carry only an entry's already-public shape metadata (name/originLanguage/kind/schema/rowCount/provenance pointer) — never raw rows. This is a DATA-LAYER batch: NO DOM, NO Room pill/avatars/toasts (that is Batch 3), and NO wiring into the SQL/Python/R tabs. Ships OFF: with this flag off nothing constructs a coordinator or calls broadcastEntry(), broadcasting only ever happens once a Room is actually joined, and every existing single-user Object Space path is byte-for-byte unchanged.

roomsUi.description: DataGlow Rooms (Batch 3 of 4): the first VISIBLE Rooms surface — the thin topbar UI that presents what Batches 1 & 2 already return, inventing no new Room concept, signaling, or broadcast payload and moving no byte of anyone's data. js/rooms/room-ui.js ships the pure, Node-testable view-model builders buildRoomPillModel() (a compact topbar 'Room pill' showing the current room code when a Room is open, a 'Start a Room' affordance when not, and an honest 'Rooms unavailable' state when WebRTC is unsupported), buildPresenceModel() (avatar/initials badges for the OTHER peers, composing Batch 1's listPeers() with Batch 2's viewingSnapshot() who's-viewing map — peerInitials()/avatarColor() give each peer stable initials + a color from a fixed palette), and buildRemoteEntryToast()/notifyRemoteEntry() (a live-update toast when a peer's Object Space entry arrives via Batch 2's receive()->onRemoteEntry, reusing the existing toast() primitive verbatim), plus the thin DOM renderer renderRoomUi() left to the browser/e2e path exactly like glow-orb-ui.js. main.js owns the Room lifecycle (renderRoomUiWidget()/startRoom()/leaveRoom()/copyRoomCode()/refreshRoomPeers()) and hands this layer already-computed state; the flag is checked only by that caller. Batch 4 (js/rooms/room-transport-adapter.js) wires the real GitHub-signaling + WebRTC adapters in, replacing the NULL no-op defaults, so with this flag ON alongside roomsSignaling/roomsBroadcast a started Room actually announces presence and exchanges live peer-to-peer data (read-only best-effort, no write token configured by default). PROMOTED to ON (this PR): renderRoomUiWidget() now renders #room-ui-host by default and the Room pill is a real, live surface for every user. Turning the flag off would fully restore the byte-for-byte-unchanged prior behavior (renderRoomUiWidget() hides #room-ui-host, tears down any coordinator) if ever needed.

queryMemory.description: Query Memory (Batch 1 of N): fingerprint every SQL/Python/R/Metric Studio run (SHA-256 hash of its normalized text + the tables/columns it touched, reusing the provenance sha256Hex primitive — no new crypto) and log WHO ran it and WHEN, so a later phase can surface a 'seen before' badge grounding trust in real validated usage history. Explicitly complementary to the AI Readiness Gate: the Gate decides agent-readiness now, Query Memory can become the audit trail behind those decisions over time. Batch 1 ships the PURE, Node-testable module js/provenance/query-memory.js only (computeQueryFingerprint / createQueryMemoryLog{record,lookup,history} / summarizeQueryMemory — exact-match semantics, documented; fuzzy/near-match deferred) plus the real append-only capped IndexedDB store (appendQueryMemory/getQueryMemory/getQueryMemoryByFingerprint/clearQueryMemory in js/learning/memory-store.js, DB v5). NO UI and NO wiring into the SQL/Python/R/Metric Studio run paths yet — same batch-1 pattern as the AI Readiness Gate and The Glow. Ships OFF: nothing in the app reads or writes the log while the flag is off.

sourceConvergence.description: Source Convergence (Batch 1 of 3): the Truth Network concept that reasons ACROSS N loaded sources at once (not just two) — given each source's rows and its possible join keys, it works out which sources describe the same real-world entity, how they overlap (coverage pattern), and where they disagree on a shared column, then resolves each conflict to the highest-trust source's value only when the trust margin is decisive, escalating the rest for human review. It supersedes the reverted two-table Cross-Table Relational Rules concept (#197/#200) with a graph-based N-way model that also follows TRANSITIVE joins (A joins B, B joins C, so A and C converge through B even with no shared key). Batch 1 ships ONLY the pure, Node-testable module js/validation/source-convergence.js: buildConvergenceGraph() (join graph + connected components incl. transitive reachability), computeConvergenceClusters() (group rows into same-entity clusters with per-column agree/conflict analysis), resolveClusterWithTrust() (trust-margin resolution with honest escalation, default marginThreshold 0.15 — the mockup's Roster·Adj 0.75 vs CMS Elig. 0.65 → margin 0.10 → escalate), and summarizeConvergence() (the '41 of 2,987 joined clusters need a human decision — 2,946 auto-resolved by trust weight' headline). It is DOM-free, DuckDB-free, network-free, no-async, and never throws — malformed/empty input returns a safe idle result. NO UI and NO ingestion wiring yet: with this flag off (its shipped default) nothing in the app imports or calls into the module, so every existing path is byte-for-byte unchanged. Batch 2 (Excel/API/site ingestion wiring) and Batch 3 (UI) are separate future PRs; dark here is intentional per the Source Convergence batching plan.

sourceConvergenceIngestion.description: Source Convergence (Batch 2 of 3): the ingestion adapters that turn the real, mixed-format data an analyst loads into the { id, rows, possibleKeys, trust, meta } source objects Batch 1's engine (js/validation/source-convergence.js) already expects — without touching that engine's public contract. js/validation/source-convergence-ingestion.js is pure, Node-testable, and consumes ALREADY-PARSED data (rows/sheets/JSON) so it needs no XLSX/DOM/network of its own: adaptExcelWorkbook() fans a parsed workbook out to ONE source PER SHEET/TAB (same file, different tabs = different sources, matching the mockup's 'Roster.xlsx → Providers' / '→ Adjustments' pattern); adaptApiSource() and adaptSiteExport() defensively unwrap a parsed JSON pull (bare array or an object wrapping rows under data/rows/results/items/records) and carry url + fetchedAt provenance; inferJoinKeys() is the shared heuristic key-inference helper (single-column *_id/_key/_code/bare-id plus known composites like patient_id+date_of_service — returns [] and flags needsManualKeySelection when nothing matches, never guessing); assignDefaultTrust() gives a sane per-origin default (upload/Excel > API > site, in the mockup's 0.65-0.92 spread) that an explicit caller trust always overrides; and toEngineSources() flattens the adapter results into the engine's source list + sourceTrust map, dropping any error-flagged source. NEVER throws — malformed/empty input returns a safe { ok:false } source (or [] for the workbook fan-out), matching the engine's never-throw discipline. Zero-upload/local-first: every adapter reads data the caller already holds client-side; the real File reading (the app's existing SheetJS XLSX.read in js/app-shell/loaders.js) and the user-initiated client-side fetch() live in the UI batch. NO UI and NO wiring into any shipping path yet — that is Batch 3. Ships OFF: with this flag off nothing imports the module, so every existing path is byte-for-byte unchanged.

sourceConvergenceUI.description: Source Convergence (Batch 3 of 3, final): the first VISIBLE Source Convergence surface — a new 'Convergence' tab that wires the already-merged Batch 1 engine (js/validation/source-convergence.js) and Batch 2 ingestion adapters (js/validation/source-convergence-ingestion.js) into a real UI, inventing no convergence logic of its own. js/validation/source-convergence-ui.js ships the pure, Node-testable view-model builders — buildConvergenceView() runs the whole pipeline (toEngineSources -> buildConvergenceGraph -> computeConvergenceClusters -> resolveClusterWithTrust per cluster -> summarizeConvergence) and returns a DOM-free model with a source rail, a coverage matrix of clusters, the plain-language verdict, and the escalate list; buildSourceCardModel(), sourceKindBadge(), formatTrust(), buildEscalationModel(), and the pure toggleExpanded() click-through state transition — plus the browser-only renderer mountConvergence() (file/tab load via the app's existing global XLSX, and user-initiated client-side fetch() for API/site pulls) left to the browser/e2e path exactly like room-ui.js/glow-orb-ui.js. The tab is gated by this ONE flag: with sourceConvergenceUI off (its shipped default) the 'convergence' tab is never added to the bar (see main.js renderTabBar), is never a dead click target, and #panel-convergence stays empty — the whole app shell is byte-for-byte unchanged. Renders an honest empty state until the analyst loads real sources; never fabricates the mockup's demo numbers. Zero-upload/local-first: every load reads data the user chose, client-side, and only ever produces a local summary. This is the final Source Convergence batch; promoting the three flags to ON is separate future work.
sourceConvergenceUI.note: flag-hygiene: sits alongside sourceConvergence (batch1) and sourceConvergenceIngestion (batch2); the trio is intended to be promoted together once the Convergence tab is dogfooded, per the Source Convergence batching plan in NORTH_STAR.md.

crucibleValidator.description: The Crucible (Batch 1 of 3, PROMOTED TO ON): an adversarial validator that stress-tests another agent's (or rule's) PROPOSED data changes BEFORE they are applied, over a typed handoff contract. Batch 1 ships two pure, Node-testable modules: js/validation/crucible-contract.js — the typed handoff objects buildCleaningResult() (the one-way handoff INTO the Crucible: a { changes[], confidence, rulesCited[], agentId } proposal) and buildValidationVerdict() (the one-way handoff OUT: a { subjectResult, packResults[], decision: 'accept'|'reject'|'escalate', escalationReason } response back to whatever orchestration layer applies or discards the changes), plus fingerprintCleaningResult() which reuses the provenance sha256Hex primitive (no new crypto) to give a result a stable content id; and js/validation/crucible-adversarial-packs.js — a library of deterministic adversarial test packs (nameOrderSwapPack, ssnTranspositionPack, boundaryDatePack, impossibleValuePack) plus runAdversarialSuite(), each pack a self-contained { id, label, category, generateCases(), evaluate(agentUnderTest, cases) } probe using fixed literal fixtures (no Math.random) so a run is reproducible. The two entity-matching packs encode the two AHIMA patient-matching failure patterns NORTH_STAR's 2026-07-12 findings confirmed the shipped fuzzy-dedup (js/cleaning/fuzzy-dedup.js) misses, and the integration test asserts they currently and correctly FAIL against the real findFuzzyDuplicates() — documenting a real gap, not a regression. Both modules are DOM-free, DuckDB-free, network-free (async only where SHA-256 requires it) and NEVER throw — malformed/empty input returns a safe { ok:false } result and an agent-under-test that throws is recorded as a failure, not a crash. Turning this flag ON makes the modules importable/usable by any caller, but is NOT user-visible on its own: Batch 2's UI tab is gated by the separate crucibleValidatorUI flag (still off), and no orchestration layer yet wires these packs into the app's real cleaning flow (Batch 3's revert-proposal module is also separately flagged and does not consume this batch's packs). No apply/revert pipeline that acts on a verdict exists — that would be new, separate future work. Turning the flag back off fully restores the byte-for-byte-unchanged prior behavior if ever needed.

crucibleValidatorUI.description: The Crucible (Batch 2 of 3, PROMOTED TO ON): the first VISIBLE Crucible surface — a new read-only 'Crucible' tab that presents the already-merged Batch 1 logic (js/validation/crucible-contract.js typed handoff objects + js/validation/crucible-adversarial-packs.js adversarial packs / runAdversarialSuite), inventing NO validation logic and adding NO new data-mutation code path. js/validation/crucible-ui.js ships the pure, Node-testable view-model builders — shouldOfferCrucible() (the flag gate); buildPipelineModel(cleaningResult, validationVerdict) which models the three-step pipeline Clean Agent → Crucible Validator → Provenance Ledger, each step carrying a status (idle | running | done) and the contract fields it reasons about (proposed-changes count / confidence / rulesCited / agentId from a CleaningResult; decision / escalationReason / packs-run from a ValidationVerdict); buildAdversarialPackListModel(packResults) which lists each pack's id/label/category and pass-fail; and buildRunLogModel(suiteResult) which turns a runAdversarialSuite() summary into a pass/fail log plus an explicit escalation callout naming the packs a human must review — plus the browser-only renderer mountCrucible() left to the browser/e2e path exactly like source-convergence-ui.js/room-ui.js. The tab is gated by this ONE flag, now ON: the 'crucible' tab appears in the bar (see main.js renderTabBar) and #panel-crucible renders. Turning the flag back off fully restores the byte-for-byte-unchanged prior behavior if ever needed. Renders an HONEST empty state (the idle 3-step pipeline skeleton + a 'nothing has been run yet' prompt); it never fabricates the mockup's demo numbers, and no orchestration feeds a live proposal through it yet. Batch 3 (crucibleRevertProposals, still off) is proposal-only — it classifies which past fixes are safely revert-eligible and describes what a revert would do, but does not execute any database mutation or wire into this UI; a live apply/revert Provenance Ledger step does not exist and would be new, separate future work. This is a SEPARATE flag from Batch 1's crucibleValidator (which gates the pure logic and is untouched here); crucibleRevertProposals remains its own separately-gated, separately-promoted flag.
crucibleValidatorUI.note: flag-hygiene: sits alongside crucibleValidator (batch1); the read-only UI depends on Batch 1's modules but on its own flag so the logic can ship/promote independently of the surface, per the Crucible batching plan in NORTH_STAR.md.

crucibleRevertProposals.description: The Crucible (Batch 3 of 3, PROMOTED TO ON): revert PROPOSALS layered on top of the existing provenance blame trail (js/provenance/data-blame.js), deliberately narrowed to a proposal-only scope after inspecting the real cleaning code (js/cleaning/clean.js applyFix). js/provenance/revert-eligibility.js ships three pure, Node-testable, DOM-free, never-throwing functions that reuse normalizeBlameEntry() as the single blame-entry shape (no parallel shape invented): classifyRevertEligibility(blameEntry) -> { eligible, reason, category } where category is 'update' | 'delete' | 'aggregate' | 'insufficient-detail'; buildRevertProposal(blameEntry) which, for an eligible entry only, returns an INERT, INSPECTABLE data description { table, column, predicate, restoreValue, sourceStepHash, humanDescription } (e.g. 'Restore NULL in column "age" where it was previously set to 0 by the fill_zero fix') and for an ineligible entry mirrors the classifier's { eligible:false, reason, category }; and summarizeRevertProposals(blameEntries) -> { eligibleCount, ineligibleCount, byCategory, proposals } over a whole trail. (a) A revert proposal is inert DATA only — this module emits NO executable SQL and NEVER mutates data; it describes an intent, nothing more. (b) DELETE-style fixes (drop_rows, dedupe) and aggregate-derived fills (fill_mean, fill_mode) are PERMANENTLY and CORRECTLY not revert-eligible: for the former the rows are gone and the trail records their removal not their contents, for the latter the written value was computed from the data at fix time so a naive undo would risk silently diverging — this is a deliberate safety choice, not a coverage gap. UPDATE-style fixes (fill_zero, abs_value, null_out, trim) are eligible ONLY when the trail actually captured usable before/after values; anything ambiguous (missing before/after, or an unrecognized rule) is conservatively treated as not eligible and never fabricated. (c) With this flag now ON, the module is importable/usable by any caller, but nothing user-visible changes yet: no UI surfaces revert proposals and no orchestration layer calls this module in the live app. Turning the flag back off fully restores the byte-for-byte-unchanged prior behavior if ever needed. (d) This closes the 3-batch Crucible build for its current scope; actually EXECUTING a revert against live DuckDB is explicitly OUT OF SCOPE — it would be brand-new, separate future work needing its own safety review, NOT an already-planned/numbered next batch.
crucibleRevertProposals.note: flag-hygiene: separate from crucibleValidator (batch1 logic) and crucibleValidatorUI (batch2 read-only tab); this batch adds pure eligibility/proposal logic only, no UI wiring and no mutation path, so it ships and promotes independently.

crucibleOrchestration.description: The Crucible (Orchestration, NEW scope beyond Batches 1–3, PROMOTED TO ON): a pure, DOM-free, never-throwing glue layer (js/validation/crucible-orchestrator.js, runCrucibleForFix) that finally connects the three already-built, already-tested Crucible modules — crucible-contract.js (buildCleaningResult/buildValidationVerdict), crucible-adversarial-packs.js (runAdversarialSuite/CRUCIBLE_PACKS), and revert-eligibility.js — so the Crucible tab can show LIVE adversarial-pack results from a real cleaning run instead of always rendering the honest empty state. (a) ADDITIVE ONLY: it hooks into main.js applyAndRecord AFTER the existing provenance.recordStep succeeds, wrapped in its own try/catch behind this flag; it never blocks, delays, or alters the cleaning-fix flow — even if the orchestration path throws or the adversarial suite reports failures, the fix has already been applied and recorded and nothing about it changes. (b) On each applied fix it builds a CleaningResult from the real fix data (the fixed column, the fix rule as the cited rule, confidence from fix-confidence.js scoreFixConfidence scaled to [0,1], agentId 'dataglow-clean-agent'), runs the full adversarial suite in STANDING-SUITE mode (one fixed reference agent assembled only from shipped primitives — fuzzy-dedup character similarity for the entity packs, a Date.UTC calendar round-trip for boundary dates, and physiological-plausibility vital bounds for impossible values — the suite reflects those primitives' honest documented capability and does not vary per fix; the name-order-swap AHIMA gap and the non-vital 'age' gap are surfaced on purpose, not tuned away), builds a ValidationVerdict, and classifies the fix's blame entry for revert-eligibility. (c) The ValidationVerdict decision is 'accept' when every pack passes and 'escalate' otherwise — NEVER 'reject': this glue layer has no authority to reject a fix a human already applied; actually acting on a verdict (auto-revert/block) is explicitly future work. (d) Now ON: every applied fix runs this path, stashing the latest run in RAM (state.latestCrucibleRun) and feeding it to the existing mountCrucible() view — no data is mutated and nothing leaves the browser. Turning the flag back off fully restores the byte-for-byte-unchanged prior cleaning-fix flow and empty-state tab if ever needed.
crucibleOrchestration.note: flag-hygiene: NEW orchestration scope, separate from crucibleValidator/crucibleValidatorUI/crucibleRevertProposals; it consumes those merged modules but adds no validation logic of its own and no mutation path. Additive-only glue — promote (remove flag, keep code) once the live tab wiring is reviewed, or revert (remove flag + code) with zero effect on the fix flow.

pluginPacks.description: Gen 40 domain-pack plugin architecture: assemble the active domain-pack source from self-contained plugin modules under js/packs/ via the pack registry instead of the inline DOMAIN_PACKS map. Behaviour-identical (same runtime pack objects); flip off to fall back to the legacy inline map.

conversationalPackBuilder.description: Gen 42 guided conversational pack builder: replace the blank-text-box pack authoring with a data-grounded question flow (js/agents/question-generator-agent.js), an on-device 'I don't know' resolver (js/agents/uncertainty-resolver-agent.js), a read-only peer-pack index (js/packs/local-pack-index.js), and an incremental pack builder (js/agents/pack-builder-agent.js). Backend (#89) + UI wiring (#91) both merged and tested; flipped on 2026-07-11 for live use in the Validate tab.

conversationalPackBuilderVoice.description: Gen 42 voice input for the conversational pack builder. The free-text answer path is fully implemented and identical to voice (voice is just transcribed text). No permissively-licensed on-device WASM/WebGPU STT model is vendored yet, so the mic is gated behind this flag with a TODO: pick a small Apache/MIT WASM speech model before promotion. When off, buildQuestionView hides the mic entirely and only the typed path shows.

metricContracts.description: Gen 44 Metric Contracts: an append-only version history for each Metric Studio definition (js/metrics/metric-contracts.js) plus a diff view (js/metrics/metric-contract-diff-view.js) and a confirm-gate so any AI-agent-proposed change to a metric's formula/name/owner/filters renders as an exact diff and requires one explicit human click before it applies — nothing auto-applies, ever. Batch 1 shipped the pure versioned data model (MetricContractHistory/MetricContractRegistry + diffVersions). Batch 2 added the read-only diff-view/history-list content builders + DOM presenter (buildDiffViewContent/buildHistoryListContent/renderDiffView). Batch 3 added the confirm gate (js/metrics/metric-contract-confirm-gate.js) — proposeContractChange/approve/reject/renderConfirmGate — the ONLY path by which an AI-agent-proposed change can ever reach recordVersion(). PROMOTED to ON (this PR): renderMetricStudio's new onDefinitionSaved hook now records a human version every time a metric is created/merged in Metric Studio, and the Validate tab shows a read-only per-metric definition-history panel (renderMetricContractHistoryPanel() in js/app-shell/main.js). The confirm gate is wired to renderConfirmGate but only surfaces if a pending AI proposal exists; no metric-touching AI proposer exists in the app today, so the gate never surfaces in normal use — it is wired, not faked. Turning the flag off fully restores the prior behavior (saves record nothing, no history panel renders).

metricAccessRules.description: Metric Contracts, Batch 4: agent-access rules — NORTH_STAR.md backlog item 2 ('Machine-readable Metric Contracts extended with agent-access rules (who/what is authorized to query a given metric) — partially exists, could be deepened'). Adds js/metrics/metric-access-rules.js: MetricAccessRule (a per-metric allow-list of agent/source identifiers, or the explicit ANY_AGENT wildcard, permitted to QUERY — read — a metric's computed value) and MetricAccessRuleRegistry (keyed by metric id, current-state not append-only — setRule() replaces rather than appends, unlike the write-side MetricContractHistory). This is a READ-time capability check, a deliberately different concept from js/agents/agent-action-firewall.js's WRITE-time mutation confirm gate — reading a metric's value is not a mutation, so the firewall's per-action human confirmation handshake does not apply here. A metric with no rule ever configured defaults to unrestricted access (ruleFor() returns an implicit ANY_AGENT rule), so shipping this module changes nothing until a rule is explicitly set for a given metric. Ships as PURE LOGIC ONLY this PR, same identity-split precedent as metric-contracts.js Batch 1: no UI yet (a future batch would add an editor inside the Metric Studio contract view), and NOT WIRED into any real query path yet (NL-SQL execution, MCP interface, Query Sentinel) — isAuthorized() is a side-effect-free predicate a future batch decides whether/how to call. Being pure logic with no caller, this flag is inert regardless of its value today; it exists so the eventual UI + wiring batch has an explicit, separate enable decision per standing convention, and so the flag-state guard test has something to track from day one. 34/34 tests pass (test/metric-access-rules.test.mjs).

dataDiplomacy.description: Data Diplomacy: reconcile two sources that disagree on the same fact, and apply the winner only behind a mandatory two-key human sign-off. With this flag ON, the Diplomacy tab is live and visible in the tab bar, backed by the real reconciliation engine + two-key approval UI shipped in PR #146 and #148. Batch 1 (#146) shipped the pure, browser-free engine: sealClaim/verifyClaimSeal (js/diplomacy/diplomacy-claim.js — inert, SHA-256-fingerprinted tamper-evident claims reusing the existing provenance sha256Hex + verifiable-check-seal canonicalJSON, no new crypto), reconcileClaims/explainReconciliation (js/diplomacy/reconciliation-engine.js — PURE, never throws; higher confidence wins beyond a tie threshold, else a caller source-trust ranking, else it HONESTLY refuses with resolved:false 'needs human debate' rather than guessing), and createApprovalRequest/approve/reject/verifyApprovalRecord (js/diplomacy/diplomacy-approval-gate.js — a two-key state machine that seals an applied record ONLY once BOTH distinct parties approve). Batch 2 (#148) added the thin presenter (js/diplomacy/diplomacy-ui.js — buildClaimCardModel/buildReconciliationPanelModel/renderDiplomacyPanel) and wired the Diplomacy tab: two claim cards side by side, the engine's verdict with its real rationale, and — ONLY when the engine resolved — a two-key approval row where each party's Approve button turns only that party's key (an unresolved verdict renders NO approval UI). The tab currently mounts a hardcoded DEMO scenario built with the real engine; Batch 3 (real data loading — wiring claims to the loaded dataset's columns and to a real cross-device transport so the two keys are held by two different people) is NOT STARTED and remains future work.

metricStudio.description: OneCanvas Phase 1 (Part 5) Metric Studio: define a named metric in plain English tied to REAL columns of the loaded dataset, compute its formula against the in-browser DuckDB engine (js/metrics/metric-studio.js), store it in a local-only in-memory + export/import JSON registry, set its status (exploratory/reviewed/certified), and get merge/keep-both prompts on >90%-similar or same-formula duplicates. PROMOTED to ON (this PR): the Metric Studio panel now renders by default in the Validate tab (renderMetricStudioPanel() in js/app-shell/main.js). Turning the flag off would fully restore the byte-for-byte-unchanged prior behavior (main.js never mounts the panel and nothing renders) if ever needed.

trustStripProofDrawer.description: OneCanvas Phase 1 (Parts 3 & 4) Trust Strip + Proof Drawer: a persistent bar at the top of the Validate tab (js/trust/trust-strip.js) whose fields — freshness, metric certification counts, validation pass/fail, anomaly indicator, lineage availability, last update — all source from real computed data, and a slide-out Proof Drawer (js/trust/proof-drawer.js) that opens scoped to a clicked field or a Metric Studio metric, reusing the existing renderAttestationHTML()/renderReceiptHTML() for provenance. PROMOTED to ON (this PR): the Trust Strip now renders by default at the top of the Validate tab (renderTrustStripPanel() in js/app-shell/main.js). Turning the flag off would fully restore the byte-for-byte-unchanged prior behavior (main.js never renders the strip and nothing shows) if ever needed.

aiReadinessGateBadge.description: AI Readiness Gate (batch 2 of 4) UI badge: a compact, INFORMATIONAL pass/fail pill (js/gate/readiness-gate-ui.js) shown below the SQL result in #sql-result-wrap. It composes the last real validation run (state.validationResults) via the pure batch-1 computeReadinessGate() into a single agent-consumability verdict — green 'Agent-ready', amber/red 'Not agent-ready' with the 0-100 score, and a click-to-expand reasons panel rendered from explainGateReasons(). It never re-runs validation, invents no new severity levels, and NEVER blocks or delays a query for a human — the hard block for agents is batch 3 (js/agents/*), not wired here. Only the SQL tab is wired in this batch; Python/R/Metric Studio and a real per-query metric-contract status are a documented follow-up. Flipped on 2026-07-12 (add-ai-readiness-gate-batch2-ui promoted): the informational badge now renders by default in the SQL tab; it remains purely informational and never blocks a human.

aiReadinessGateEnforcement.description: AI Readiness Gate (batch 3 of 4) AGENT hard-block enforcement: when on, DataGlow's own data-consuming agent modules (js/agents/question-generator-agent.js and js/agents/uncertainty-resolver-agent.js) refuse to produce output from a dataset/query result that the pure batch-1 computeReadinessGate() marks agentConsumable:false, returning a graceful refusal built from explainGateReasons() instead. It blocks ONLY js/agents/* automated output — never a human. Human-facing SQL/Python/R/Metric Studio workflows are entirely unaffected in both flag states. Enforcement is threaded through main.js's conversational pack builder ONLY when this flag is on (it passes the already-computed validation results as layerResults — no re-run of validation). Ships dark: with the flag off (its shipped default) the agents behave exactly as before this PR (no gate call, no refusal) and no human or agent path changes.

multiDialectSql.description: Polyglot Workbench (Batch A) multi-dialect SQL translation: a pure, standalone adapter (js/app-shell/sql-dialect-adapter.js) that transpiles SQL written in PostgreSQL, MySQL, BigQuery, Snowflake, or T-SQL into the DuckDB SQL the in-browser engine runs, via a small set of composable, literal-safe regex/string rules (no full parser). translateDialectSql(sql, dialect) is a pure string->string function (no DOM, no DuckDB import) and SUPPORTED_DIALECTS drives the picker. When on, the SQL tab shows a dialect-picker chip row above the editor and runs translateDialectSql(userSql, selectedDialect) before handing SQL to runQuery; the default selection is DuckDB (a byte-for-byte no-op passthrough). When off (its shipped default), no picker renders and the SQL tab runs the user's SQL exactly as before this PR. Batch B (an Object Space registry) is a separate, not-yet-started follow-up PR.

localAnalysisContract.description: Local Analysis Contract (js/validation/analysis-contract.js): before/alongside running a SQL query — the user's own, or one an AI assistant wrote — checks it offline against the REAL schema of the loaded dataset and flags schema hallucination (references to columns/tables that don't exist), aggregation mismatches (COUNT without DISTINCT across a JOIN; SUM of a rate-like column), and missing guard clauses (aggregate query never excludes test/deleted/refunded rows a matching column suggests it should). Flags-only, never rewrites or blocks a query — empowerment-constraint compliant. Renders as a dismissible card in #sql-result-wrap, above the result table. When off, the SQL tab behaves exactly as before this PR.

meetingScribe.description: Gen 43 meeting scribe: pure grounding/tagging logic (js/agents/meeting-scribe-agent.js, PR gen43-meeting-scribe-agent) plus a Meeting tab presenter (js/agents/meeting-scribe-ui.js, PR gen43-meeting-scribe-ui) where a person pastes or types a transcript to see pushback moments, data requests, and an action-item tracker enforcing the owner+dueDate+outcome rule. Still no audio capture or speech-to-text — those stay separate follow-up work. When this flag is off (its shipped default), renderTabBar() leaves the 'meeting' entry out of state.tabOrder entirely, so the tab is not just hidden but never added to the bar or reachable by any click path; #panel-meeting stays empty.

meetingScribeLiveCapture.description: DataGlow Live Rooms (Batch 1) live audio-capture + on-device speech-to-text input for the Meeting Scribe: the follow-up the Gen 43 meeting-scribe PR deliberately deferred. Pure, Node-testable logic lives in js/agents/live-transcript-capture.js — isSpeechCaptureAvailable() (a never-throwing capability check mirroring ondevice-llm.js's isWebGPUAvailable(): false unless BOTH navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia AND WebGPU are present) plus assembleSegments()/createTranscriptAssembler(), which turn raw STT chunks (interim vs. final text + timestamps) into the SAME {text, ts} segment shape parseTranscriptText produces, so they feed directly into the EXISTING, unchanged tagSegmentsWithContext. The browser-only half (getUserMedia mic capture + a lazily-CDN-loaded on-device WebGPU Whisper-family STT pipeline via transformers.js) is code-loaded from a CDN like WebLLM in ondevice-llm.js and NEVER routes microphone audio anywhere. When ON, mountMeetingScribe (js/agents/meeting-scribe-ui.js) renders an ADDITIONAL 'Start live capture' / 'Stop' button pair beside the paste-a-transcript textarea; captured segments stream into the SAME taggedSegments state and re-render through the exact same renderResultsInto path as a pasted transcript. When OFF (its shipped default), main.js passes liveCapture:false, so NONE of the live-capture UI renders and the paste-a-transcript flow behaves byte-for-byte as before this PR — zero behavior change. This flag is INTENTIONALLY default OFF/dark per the DataGlow Live Rooms batching plan (Batches 2-4 — device pairing/WebRTC, chart-context timeline wiring, and on-device-LLM synthesis — are separate future PRs); dark here is expected, not an oversight.

meetingDecisionLedger.description: Gen 43 meeting decision ledger: a permanent, append-only, chart-anchored local record of pushback moments, data requests, and action items surfaced by the meeting scribe (js/agents/meeting-decision-ledger.js pure logic, persisted via js/learning/memory-store.js's new meetingDecisionLedger IndexedDB object store, presented by js/agents/meeting-decision-ledger-ui.js). A separate flag from meetingScribe so this piece can ship dark independently of that flag's state. Nothing is saved automatically — the analyst must click 'Save this meeting to ledger' for anything to be written; browsing, filtering by chart/type, exporting to a local .json file, and clearing the ledger are all explicit, on-device actions with no network call anywhere in this feature. When this flag is off (its shipped default), #meeting-decision-ledger-body stays empty and the section is never mounted.

personalDataBom.description: Personal Data Bill of Materials (js/provenance/data-bom.js): a one-click, fully offline 'ingredient label' for the active dataset — composes the existing provenance attestation (js/provenance/provenance.js, unmodified) with a schema-shape signature, the column-distribution snapshot already computed for drift/validation (if one has been run), and — only when the user has explicitly loaded it — the local on-device AI model's exact id/quantization. Exports as signed-digest JSON or a printable HTML certificate via the same Export/Attestation card and download path the existing Export Attestation button uses. Same honest-labelling convention as provenance.js: a self-computed SHA-256 digest, never a claimed third-party notarization/signature. Empowerment-constraint compliant — this is a read-only export of what the user already has loaded; it never modifies data or triggers any automated action.

semanticDriftWatchdog.description: Turns the existing distribution_drift validation layer result into de-duplicated, non-spammy drift alerts on the Watch Folder panel, so an automatically re-validated file surfaces a NEW drift finding once instead of re-showing the same alert on every poll. Computes no new statistics of its own — js/ambient/drift-watchdog.js is a pure presentation + de-duplication layer over results validation.js's distribution_drift layer (and its forecast-based drift alerting) already produces on every run. A native (Tauri/Rust, OS-level file-event) trigger for the desktop shell is a deliberate, tracked follow-up — see docs/tech-debt-tracker.md — not part of this flag; today this only activates the browser-only Watch Folder poll path.

dataglowSidebarNav.description: Command Deck, Part 1: an alternate left-sidebar nav (#command-deck-sidebar, js/app-shell/command-deck-nav.js) that regroups the existing 13 tabs into 5 Trust-Tier Lifecycle Stages (Frame/Work/Trust/Generate/Tell) with collapsible stage headers, instead of the flat top tab bar. Pure reorganization of existing tabs -- zero new logic, zero new panels; every tab id is checked at test time against the real TAB_META in main.js so this can never silently drift out of sync with the app's actual 13 tools. Ships dark: with the flag off (default) the sidebar host stays hidden and the existing top tab bar remains the one and only nav, unchanged. Command Deck decision (direction / scope / naming) resolved by the agent per the user's 'build all, safely and smartly' instruction -- see docs/capability-map.md and AGENTS.md for the full stated rationale. Part 2 (command palette) and Part 3 (adaptive next-step rail) are follow-on batches, not included here.

semanticMetricsLayer.description: Semantic / Metrics Layer (js/validation/semantic-layer.js, Trust Passport Batch 1): a local, in-memory dictionary of human-authored metric definitions (name + canonical SQL expression + description + required columns + owner/createdAt provenance) plus a pattern-based comparator. Adds a FOURTH Local Analysis Contract finding class — metric_definition_mismatch — that flags when a query aliases/comments a result as a registered metric (e.g. SUM(amount) AS net_revenue) but its expression differs from the registered canonical definition (e.g. SUM(amount) - SUM(refund_amount)), naming the likely missing term. Not AI-powered: a plain registry + string/pattern comparator, no SQL AST, no model, no network. Flags-only, never rewrites/blocks/auto-authors — empowerment-constraint compliant. A minimal 'Define a metric' affordance in the SQL tab (js/validation/semantic-layer-ui.js) lets a human register a definition. Gated so the fourth check only runs when runAnalysisContract is passed a metric registry (main.js supplies one only when this flag is on); when off, the Contract behaves exactly as today (three finding classes only) and the Define-a-metric affordance is not shown. Registry is in-memory only, resets on reload — portable export/import is deferred to a later batch.

groupedNavigation.description: Groups the flat top-level tabs into 5 named modes — Explore (Problem Framer, Preflight), Validate & Trust (Clean, Validate, Diff), Analyze (SQL, Python, R), Visualize & Share (Visualize, Story), Automate (Digital Twin, Watch Folder) — rendered by js/app-shell/tab-groups.js's pure buildTabGroups(tabOrder)/groupForTab(tabId) reducer. This is a presentation-only change: no tab id, panel id, switchTab() call, drag-reorder handler, or flag gate for any individual tab is touched — renderTabBar() just renders the SAME state.tabOrder ids grouped under mode headers instead of in one flat row, and clicking a tab does exactly what it did before. PROMOTED to ON (this PR): the grouped tab bar is now the default for every user. Turning the flag off would fully restore the byte-for-byte-unchanged prior behavior (renderTabBar() renders the original flat single-row bar) if ever needed.

validateFocusMode.description: Declutters the Validate tab down to one primary action (Run All 20 Layers) by wrapping its secondary controls (context input, domain pack picker, level picker, export/import/receipt buttons) plus the AI Synthesis and Peer Review Mode cards in <details> disclosures, closed by default. js/app-shell/validate-focus.js's pure shouldExpandAdvanced({hasRunOnce, wasManuallyExpanded}) and createValidateFocusStore() decide open/closed state per dataset, in memory only — never persisted. A dataset's Advanced options auto-open the first time validation is run on it, or if the user manually opens any one of the three (they then track together). PROMOTED to ON (this PR): the decluttered Validate layout is now the default for every user. Turning the flag off would fully restore the byte-for-byte-unchanged prior behavior (applyValidateFocusMode() forces every one of these <details> open on every render — the pre-PR fully-expanded layout) if ever needed.

dataNutritionLabel.description: Trust Passport (Batch 2) Data Nutrition Label: a portable provenance manifest (js/provenance/data-nutrition-label.js) that knits the existing chain-of-custody trail, Assumption Ledger, and per-layer validation results into ONE self-describing summary — what was checked, what passed/failed, what transformations were recorded, any assumptions, whether the data is synthetic, and the chain of custody — that a recipient can inspect without running DATAGLOW. It is a summary/manifest only, NOT a certification or verification. Opt-in only: with the flag on, the Export card on the Visualize tab shows a 'Include Data Nutrition Label' checkbox; only when a human ticks it does the export append a human-readable label section (PDF/Excel) and download the machine-readable .json manifest alongside the existing export. When this flag is off (its shipped default), the checkbox is hidden and the export flow is byte-for-byte unchanged.

verifiableCheckSeal.description: Trust Passport (Batch 3) Verifiable Check Seal ("Proof-of-Clean"): seals a validation check result (e.g. a Local Analysis Contract run) into a portable Merkle-tree (SHA-256) commitment (js/provenance/verifiable-check-seal.js) that APPLIES the existing js/provenance/selective-disclosure-proof.js primitive — no new crypto. The seal lets a third party confirm 'a check with these parameters ran against data matching this exact SHA-256 fingerprint and produced this result' WITHOUT seeing the underlying data, and it can be attached additively to a batch-2 Data Nutrition Label's custodyChain (new custodyChain.seals array; no existing field changed). verifySeal genuinely re-checks: it re-folds every sealed claim to the committed Merkle root (detects any altered value) and, when data is supplied, re-fingerprints it so modified data fails to match — it is a real tamper check, not a 'verified' label. HONEST NAMING: it is NOT a zero-knowledge proof (parameters/result/fingerprints are cleartext), NOT a certification, NOT 'blockchain'; it does not attest the data is accurate or truthful, only that the check ran against the fingerprinted data and produced this result. Opt-in only: sealing is always an explicit human action (a 'Seal this result' button in the SQL tab's Analysis Contract flow), never automatic. When this flag is off (its shipped default), no seal UI renders and behaviour is exactly as before this PR.

syntheticDataPassport.description: Trust Passport (Batch 4, the finale) Governed Synthetic Data Passport: js/privacy/synthetic-data-passport.js COMPOSES batches 1-3 so a synthetic export never leaves 'naked'. buildSyntheticDataPassport() wraps batch 2's buildDataNutritionLabel (isSynthetic=true) with a `synthetic` block that HONESTLY describes how the data was generated and what privacy guarantee applies — a formal differential-privacy claim (mechanism + a specific ε) is asserted ONLY when the generation context actually establishes one (the Synthetic Twin and the DP aggregate export both do, via the Laplace mechanism), and the generator's own disclaimer is carried verbatim; where the method is heuristic / declares no DP the passport says plainly that no formal guarantee applies and the output may be re-identifiable. A DP budget is never upgraded into 'anonymized' / HIPAA Safe Harbor. sealSyntheticPassport() OPT-IN seals the exact generation parameters bound to a fingerprint of the synthetic OUTPUT via batch 3 and attaches it additively to the label custodyChain. Opt-in only: with the flag on, the Synthetic Twin card shows an 'Include Governance Passport' checkbox and, after generating, a 'Download Passport (.json)' / 'Seal + download' pair; only an explicit human click builds, seals, or downloads anything. When this flag is off (its shipped default), no passport UI renders and the synthetic-export flow is byte-for-byte unchanged.

objectSpaceRegistry.description: Polyglot Workbench (Batch B) Object Space registry: a shared, in-memory read model (js/app-shell/object-space.js — createObjectSpace/registerObject/listObjectSpace) that tracks the named objects living across the SQL (DuckDB), Python (Pyodide/pandas), and R (WebR) runtimes as one source of truth — each entry records name, originLanguage ('sql'|'python'|'r'), kind ('dataframe'|'model'|'scalar'), schema [{name,type}], rowCount, createdAt, and a provenance pointer into the existing chain-of-custody registry (no duplication of provenance.js logic). ADDITIVE and passive: it sits ALONGSIDE the existing per-language JSON round-trip bridges (dataglow.get_df / dataglow_get_df / FROM <table>) and does NOT replace their transfer mechanics, nor resolve cross-language references at query time (no working `FROM py.name` yet — a deliberate future batch). When on, the runtime wiring in js/app-shell/main.js additionally calls registerObject(...) after a SQL query result, and after each Python/R run for every dataset the bridge already exposed, and a small read-only 'Object Space' strip renders in the data sidebar listing live objects with an origin-language badge and row count/kind. When this flag is off (its shipped default), zero behavior changes anywhere — no registerObject call fires and no Object Space UI renders. Batch A (the multi-dialect SQL adapter) is a separate PR.

trustBeam.description: Trust Beam (Trust Passport): turns an existing Verifiable Check Seal (js/provenance/verifiable-check-seal.js) into a self-contained, URL-safe shareable link whose whole payload lives in the URL FRAGMENT (after '#'), so nothing is ever sent to or stored on a server. js/provenance/trust-beam.js is a pure, dependency-free serializer — encodeBeam(seal) base64url-encodes the seal verbatim inside a versioned envelope, decodeBeam reverses it losslessly, and buildBeamUrl(seal, baseUrl) composes the full link; it adds NO crypto and NO trust of its own (it is a transport wrapper — every property, and every honest limit, of the underlying seal carries through unchanged). A standalone static page (verify-beam.html) opened by a recipient with ZERO DataGlow install reads the fragment, calls decodeBeam then the EXISTING verifySeal(), and renders a plain-language Verified / Tampered result fully client-side — no server, no app shell, no login, nothing uploaded. HONEST NAMING is inherited from the seal: NOT a zero-knowledge proof, NOT a certification, NOT 'blockchain'; it proves only that the check ran against data matching the committed fingerprint and produced this result, not that the data is accurate. Opt-in only: with the flag on, a 'Beam it' button appears next to the existing 'Seal this result' button in the SQL tab's Analysis Contract flow (js/app-shell/main.js), building a copyable link (QR-image rendering was considered and intentionally rejected — the copyable link is sufficient; no QR library should be vendored for this feature). When this flag is off (its shipped default), no Beam UI renders and behaviour is exactly as before this PR.

glowOrb.description: The Glow (Batches 1 & 2 of 2): one glowing topbar orb that composes DataGlow's four existing real trust/health outputs — the AI Readiness Gate verdict (computeReadinessGate), the Trust Strip field states (collectTrustSignals), the Golden Signals data-quality rates (computeGoldenSignals), and the CAT Scorecard letter grades (computeCATScore) — into a single at-a-glance verdict so the analyst doesn't have to hunt across four surfaces. Batch 1 shipped the pure, Node-testable aggregator js/glow/glow-signal.js (computeGlowSignal composes those outputs into one { status, score, signals[], nextAction, summary } object; explainGlowSignal renders it as a multi-line string). Batch 2 (this PR) adds the thin UI: js/glow/glow-orb-ui.js — buildGlowOrbModel (pure, DOM-free view-model reusing the Trust Strip's ok/warn/bad/idle dot colors verbatim) + renderGlowOrb (a ~30px circular orb button whose dot/ring reflects status; click toggles an inline panel listing status/score, each composed signal as a label/value row, an honest next-action callout, and a 'Show the math' toggle revealing explainGlowSignal()'s raw text) — wired into the topbar (#glow-orb-host, leftmost in .topbar-right) by main.js's renderGlowOrbWidget(), which composes the SAME already-computed state the Readiness Gate badge and Trust Strip use (no re-run of validation). Mirrors readiness-gate.js's compose-don't-recompute discipline: the gate score/agentConsumable is authoritative when present, every signal traces to a real field, nothing is fabricated, and it never throws (missing input → honest idle orb). Golden Signals / CAT Scorecard are computed async inside renderDataHealth and not persisted to state, so they are left undefined in the topbar composition rather than re-run synchronously — a documented Batch-2 follow-up. A future hold-to-unfold orb gesture is NOT built (click-to-expand only). Batch 3 (this PR): promoted enabled: true after dogfooding — the orb is now visible by default in the topbar. renderGlowOrbWidget() still empties #glow-orb-host and returns if the flag is ever set back to false, so it remains a safe kill-switch.

glowPathRail.description: Glow Path (Batch A) adaptive next-action rail: a single, honest 'what should I do next?' suggestion rail rendered into #glow-path-host, between the tab bar and the main area. The pure decision function (js/app-shell/glow-path.js — computeGlowPathState) composes fields the caller assembles from real state (dataset loaded/validated, validationSummary pass/warn/fail counts, an OPTIONAL real computeReadinessGate() output, and a repeat-query count) into one next action by a documented first-match-wins priority: (1) load a file, (2) run Validate, (3) an agent-readiness block explained from the gate's OWN failing layers (never fabricated; humans always still see everything), (4) review warnings, (5) save a repeated query (mid/high density only), (6) nothing actionable → renders nothing. The thin presenter (js/app-shell/glow-path-ui.js — buildGlowPathBadgeModel/renderGlowPath + an in-memory per-dataset createGlowPathDismissalStore) draws a dismissible rail, or NOTHING when there's no message. ADDITIVE alongside (not replacing) the tab bar, Command Deck sidebar, and tab groups. It NEVER re-runs validation or the gate — it reuses the output main.js already computed — and never blocks a human. densityLevel comes from the real session-scoped proficiency tracker wired in Batch C (js/learning/proficiency-signal.js), fed by recordAction calls on the sql/python/r/validate run paths — defaults to 'low' for a brand-new session and rises to 'mid'/'high' as real usage accumulates. PROMOTED to ON (Batch D): this is now LIVE for every user by default. Turning the flag off would fully restore the byte-for-byte-unchanged prior behavior (main.js never calls the rail, #glow-path-host stays empty) if ever needed.

proofRoom.description: Proof Room (Trust Passport, composition batch 1): a single new tab that composes FIVE already-shipped, already-tested trust surfaces into one top-to-bottom 'assembled proof' screen — (1) Metric Studio (js/metrics/metric-studio.js), (2) Trust Strip (js/trust/trust-strip.js), (3) Data Nutrition Label (js/provenance/data-nutrition-label.js), (4) Verifiable Check Seal (js/provenance/verifiable-check-seal.js), and (5) Trust Beam (js/provenance/trust-beam.js, with verify-beam.html as the standalone recipient verifier). Pure UI composition: NO new crypto, NO new validation logic, NO backend, NO new AI model — the tab reuses each module's existing exported render/build functions verbatim (js/provenance/proof-room.js holds only a pure step-ordering + readiness aggregator and a thin DOM presenter). This flag gates ONLY the visibility of the new composed tab; it does NOT change the five underlying flags (metricStudio/trustStripProofDrawer/dataNutritionLabel/verifiableCheckSeal/trustBeam), which each carry their own shipped default. Because the Proof Room tab is itself the opt-in surface (reachable only when this flag is on), it calls each module's pure/render function directly regardless of that module's own flag state — none of those render functions perform an internal flag check; flag gating is exclusively the caller's job. HONEST NAMING inherited from the composed modules: the assembled screen is NOT a certification, NOT 'blockchain', and NOT a zero-knowledge proof — it only summarizes checks that ran against the fingerprinted data. PROMOTED to ON (Batch 3): the Proof Room tab is now LIVE in the tab bar by default for every user — renderTabBar()'s visibleTabOrder filter now keeps the 'proofroom' entry once this flag is on, and renderProofRoomTab() assembles the five steps as soon as a dataset is loaded (before a dataset it shows a single 'Load a dataset to assemble its Proof Room' prompt). All five composed underlying flags (metricStudio + trustStripProofDrawer promoted in #170; dataNutritionLabel + verifiableCheckSeal + trustBeam promoted earlier) are confirmed ON, so every one of the five steps renders REAL content rather than an empty/placeholder state. Turning this flag back off fully restores the prior byte-for-byte-unchanged behavior — the 'proofroom' entry drops out of the rendered tab list, the tab is never a dead click target, and #panel-proofroom stays empty — so it remains a safe kill-switch.

validationExtendedCoverage.description: Extended-coverage validation checks that close two verified detection gaps (NORTH_STAR 2026-07-12 P0). (1) Cross-Column Logical Consistency (js/validation/cross-column-consistency.js) gains Rule 1c 'semantic magnitude ordering': a small explicit config of known lesser≤greater numeric-column semantics (allowed≤billed, paid≤allowed, paid≤billed) — modeled on the healthcare Domain Physics pack's hand-written domain config — that catches e.g. a claim's allowed amount exceeding its billed amount, which the existing min/max-keyword Rule 1b never paired. (2) The Unit Test Layer (js/validation/validation.js) gains a business-key duplicate check: it groups by the identifier subset of columns (detectBusinessKeyColumns — *_id/_key/_code/_no/_num/_number and bare id/key/code) and reports only groups that repeat the key while differing in a non-key column (e.g. an incidental ingest timestamp), so it never double-reports the pre-existing byte-identical duplicate check, which is left untouched. Both add NEW findings the Validate tab surfaces to users, so per the flag-if-visible-behavior-change convention this ships OFF by default: with it off, runCrossColumnChecks is called with { magnitude:false } (Rule 1c never fires) and the business-key branch is skipped entirely, restoring byte-for-byte prior Validate output. The pure detection helpers (detectMagnitudePairs, detectBusinessKeyColumns) are always exported and unit-tested directly regardless of flag state. Flip on to activate both checks.

crossTableReferentialIntegrity.description: Closes NORTH_STAR 2026-07-15 (Run 5 test) finding 0b: the Unit Test Layer's own LAYER_DEFS description has always claimed 'referential integrity' as one of its 5 silent tests, but the existing check (js/validation/validation.js runUnitTests, fkCols loop) only ever verified a foreign-key-shaped column (e.g. patient_id on a claims table) is non-NULL WITHIN the same table — it never checked whether that value actually exists as a key in another loaded table, so a syntactically-valid but nonexistent FK (e.g. a claim referencing patient_id 'PT9999' when no such patient was ever loaded) was invisible to it. This flag gates a new, genuine cross-table check: findReferenceCandidate() conservatively matches each FK-shaped column against any OTHER currently-loaded dataset (state.datasets) — by exact column-name match against that table's own first/key column, or by the FK's base noun matching the other dataset's name — and only when a candidate is found, runs a real anti-join (NOT EXISTS) query counting non-null FK values that don't exist in the candidate table's key column. Deliberately its OWN flag rather than piggybacking on validationExtendedCoverage (already enabled:true in production): this is genuinely new user-visible behavior (a new finding kind, 'orphan_reference') and per the flag-if-visible-behavior-change convention it needs its own explicit enable decision, not a silent ride-along on an already-live flag. Fails open: if the candidate table can't be joined for any reason (type mismatch, dropped mid-run) the check is skipped for that column only — the rest of the Unit Test Layer's findings are never lost. With this flag off (its shipped default) state.datasets is never read from inside runUnitTests and no new query runs, so Validate output is byte-for-byte unchanged from before this PR. This does NOT replace or overlap with Source Convergence (js/validation/source-convergence.js) — Source Convergence is a much heavier N-way entity-resolution/trust-reconciliation engine for a dedicated Convergence tab; this is the narrow, honest fix to what the Unit Test Layer itself already claims to do on the datasets a user has simply loaded and run Validate on.

agentActionFirewall.description: DataGlow Passport (Batch 1) Agent Action Firewall (js/agents/agent-action-firewall.js): the single central gate every data-MUTATING code path must pass through. It classifies an action's risk/reversibility and FAILS CLOSED — a mutation runs only after an explicit per-action human confirmation that also carries an authenticated local human identity (the audit rider); there is no trusted-mode/force/auto bypass parameter anywhere. This flag ships ENABLED by default (not dark): a safety gate that is off by default protects nothing, so unlike this repo's usual land-dark convention, the Clean-tab fix handler in main.js routes through the firewall from the start, recording who authorized each mutation into the provenance chain. Turning the flag off restores the prior direct clean.applyFix call path exactly as before this PR, as an explicit escape hatch, not the shipped default. Direct regression for the April 2026 'AI agent deleted the prod DB in 9 seconds with no confirmation' incident (test/agent-action-firewall.test.mjs).

robustnessVerdict.description: Analysis-robustness thickening (js/analysis-robustness/robustness-verdict.js): two pure functions that EXTEND Devil's Advocate. mapAssumptionSensitivity finds the smallest set of rows whose removal makes an A-vs-B gap disappear/reverse and names the segment they concentrate in; robustnessVerdict folds attackAnalysis + that sensitivity map into ONE fixed-vocabulary object {verdict:'robust'|'fragile'|'inconclusive', reason, drivingFactor} with a plain-English, number-grounded one-sentence reason. When on, the existing Devil's Advocate card in the SQL tab appends the plain verdict line + the driving-segment sensitivity summary beneath its existing checks. Ships dark: with the flag off (default) initDevilsAdvocate renders exactly as before and the new functions never run. Pure logic only — the future stakeholder-facing 'Reverse Data Story' surface is explicitly out of scope.

dataglowCommandPalette.description: Command Deck, Part 2: a global command palette (Ctrl/Cmd+K) built on js/app-shell/command-palette.js -- pure fuzzy-match + ranking logic (buildCommandList/scoreCommand/filterCommands) with zero DOM/runtime dependencies, wired into a modal in main.js. Lets a person jump to any real tab or trigger a small static registry of common in-tool actions (run query, run validation, scan for cleaning issues, run preflight, run diagnostics, export as an Excel workbook) without leaving the keyboard. Tab commands are built from the real TAB_META/tabOrder at call time, so the palette can never silently drift out of sync with the app's actual tool list. Action commands with a `whenTab` are only offered while that tab is active (e.g. 'Run SQL query' only appears on the SQL tab); actions with no `whenTab` are always offered. Ships dark: with the flag off (default) no keyboard listener is attached and no modal exists in the DOM. Independent of dataglowSidebarNav (Part 1) -- either flag can be on/off without affecting the other.

portableReceipts.description: DataGlow Passport Batch B Portable Receipts (js/provenance/portable-receipt.js): an opt-in, per-artifact lineage stamp for a SINGLE exported chart / KPI / table (not the whole-dataset validation receipt). buildClaimReceipt commits the certified claim, a content hash of the query/transform chain, the dataset validation state at compute time, and a timestamp into a Merkle (SHA-256) tree reusing the selective-disclosure-proof primitives; verifyClaimReceipt recomputes them; renderReceiptVerifierHTML emits a self-contained, offline HTML page whose inline verifier re-checks the fingerprint with zero network. When on, the Visualize and Export tabs show an explicit 'Attach Portable Receipt' checkbox that downloads a second <stem>.receipt.html file next to the artifact. Ships DARK (land-dark per the Build Nervous System convention): this is a user-facing export option, not a silent safety layer, so it stays behind the flag until the checkbox UI is verified live in a browser, then gets flipped on. Flip on to expose the opt-in receipt controls; when off, both checkboxes stay hidden and export behaves exactly as before.

openFloorKernel.description: DataGlow Open Floor, Batch A: the read-only room kernel (js/agents/open-floor-room.js) that wraps an already-loaded dataset + its governed validation/metric state behind a surface with NO mutating methods (read-only by construction, frozen, reads delegate to an injected engine reader and non-read SQL is refused fail-closed), plus a pre-submit PHI/sensitive-field prompt guard (js/agents/phi-prompt-guard.js) that redacts protected content before any text reaches the WebLLM narrative/story path or a future NL-to-SQL path. The guard reuses the existing domain-pack sensitive-category classifier (isSensitiveCategory in js/validation/categorical-consistency.js) for column names AND runs a minimal always-on value scan (SSN/MRN/email/long-id shapes) so it protects even when no domain pack is active. Pure logic only — no UI, no call site — so with this flag off (its shipped default) the app's runtime behaviour is unchanged.

ownershipLedger.description: DataGlow Passport Batch D Ownership Ledger (js/provenance/ownership-ledger.js): answers WHO is/was responsible for a dataset — distinct from the Chain of Custody, which tracks WHAT changed. deriveOwnershipEvents reads the identities ALREADY attached to the existing provenance trail + assumption ledger (it never fabricates one; today most events legitimately degrade to 'unattributed' because the identity rider is optional) into an ordered, append-only event list; summarizeCurrentOwnership infers the likely current owner by recency-weighted vote and reports 'unknown' honestly when nothing is attributed; claimOwnership is the ONLY write path — an opt-in, append-only explicit claim that requires a real local identity and never overwrites a prior claim. When on, the Provenance/Trust tab shows an 'Ownership Ledger' panel with the inferred current-owner summary, an inferred/claimed event timeline, and a 'Claim ownership' button. Ships DARK (land-dark): this is passive read-only inference plus an opt-in local write, not a safety gate, so it stays behind the flag until the panel is verified live in a browser, then gets flipped on. When off, the panel stays hidden and the tab behaves exactly as before.

trustCertificate.description: Rigor Engine Batch 4: Download Trust Certificate button (js/trust/trust-certificate.js) wired into the Preflight Trust tab. Bundles the full 20+ layer validation run, AI Readiness Gate score, and k-anonymity check into one SHA-256-signed .dataglow-cert.json file any stakeholder or downstream AI agent can verify offline without re-running the analysis. Ships live (enabled:true) — pure composition over already-shipped modules, no new validation or signing machinery.

provenancePacket.description: Provenance Packet Batch 3: the portable, signed .dataglow.json packet format (js/provenance/provenance-packet.js) plus its Export Packet / Import Packet UI in the Trust tab. Bundles whatever provenance artifacts have been produced for the loaded dataset — cell-level data blame (batch 1), the de-identification attestation (batch 1), and the denial-risk profile + cost-of-bad-data estimate (batch 2) — into one SHA-256-signed document, and imports + verifies one WITHOUT the original dataset present. Ships DARK because the section producers (PR #97, #100) are not merged to main yet; promote once they land so the Export path can gather live sections.

openFloorSandboxTwin.description: DataGlow Open Floor, Batch B: the Sandbox Twin (js/simulation/sandbox-twin.js) — a forkable, disposable, deep-copied in-memory twin of an already-loaded dataset that an autonomous agent (or a stakeholder on the Open Floor) can propose destructive ops against FREELY, because two guarantees hold at once: (1) fork isolation — mutating the twin only ever rewrites the twin's own row array and reset() restores the fork baseline, so every twin op is reversible and the real dataset is never reachable by reference; (2) nothing applies without the firewall — EVERY mutation of the twin AND, especially, promoting a twin result back into the REAL dataset routes through the Agent Action Firewall's mandatory per-action human-confirmation + single-use-nonce + identity handshake (no trusted/auto/force path). It reuses perturbRows from js/simulation/digital-twin.js and diffRows/detectKeyColumn from js/simulation/time-travel-diff.js (owns no diffing logic). DEPENDS ON the agentActionFirewall capability (js/agents/agent-action-firewall.js, PR gen44/#114): if that module is unavailable the twin degrades gracefully to a disabled, fail-closed state (console.warn, applies nothing) rather than throwing. Pure logic only — no UI, no call site — so with this flag off (its shipped default) runtime behaviour is unchanged. Red-team regression: test/sandbox-twin.test.mjs.

guardedCopilot.description: Guarded Copilot, Batch 1 (deterministic core): a read-only, lineage-citing chat-answer engine (js/agents/guarded-copilot.js) that composes the EXISTING AI Readiness Gate (js/gate/readiness-gate.js), the AI Touch Ledger (js/provenance/ai-touch-ledger.js — also used to log Guarded Copilot's own queries), and the Story engine's grade vocabulary into deterministic Tier 1 answers for five supported questions (readiness/why-blocked, why-low-confidence, what-changed, who-touched-this, explain-grade).
